original_hypothesis.title: BridgeCreator.createBridge Permissionless Deployment Enables Fake Bridge Infrastructure
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: BridgeCreator.createBridge is an external function with no access control. Anyone can deploy a complete bridge infrastructure (Bridge, SequencerInbox, Inbox, Outbox, RollupEventInbox) pointing to an arbitrary rollup address. These contracts are fully initialized and functional. An attacker could deploy bridge infrastructure pointing to a legitimate rollup address, creating convincing-looking but attacker-controlled bridge contracts that could be used to mislead users or integrations.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The permissionless createBridge deploys and initializes 5 proxy contracts. While these don't affect the legitimate rollup (the rollup's bridge is separately managed), the attacker-created bridge would have valid initialization and emit standard events, potentially confusing off-chain systems that monitor for bridge deployments.

proof_of_signal.pattern: createBridge external function with no access control modifier, deploys and initializes 5 proxy contracts with caller-supplied rollup address

preconditions: BridgeCreator is deployed and has valid templates set
Attacker knows a legitimate rollup address to reference

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: No access control on createBridge; any address can deploy full bridge infrastructure
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Deploy convincing bridge infrastructure pointing to legitimate rollup, deceive users into interacting with attacker-controlled contracts
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Stolen deposits from users interacting with fake bridge

economic_questions: Do off-chain systems verify bridge contract legitimacy via rollup governance?
Could fake bridge contracts be used to intercept deposits?
Is there a way to distinguish legitimate from fake bridge deployments on-chain?
validation_requirements: Deploy fake bridge infrastructure and verify initialization succeeds
Assess if fake bridge can receive user deposits
Check if legitimate rollup rejects messages from unauthorized bridge

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas costs for deploying 5 proxies (~0.1 ETH)
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to the total value deposited by deceived users
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: High if victims interact with fake bridge
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single deployment transaction, then ongoing deception

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Potential user fund theft via social engineering

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users tricked into using fake bridge contracts
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of deposits made to fake bridge

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify fake bridge initialization and interaction patterns

validation_result.impact_summary: Filtered before validation: attack_realism is LOW
validation_result.detailed_analysis: This hypothesis was auto-rejected because its attack_realism grade is LOW. Preconditions require actions outside the attacker's control (off-chain compromise, victim cooperation, or admin collusion). No LLM validation was performed.
